concepts.topic: Describe different types of supply chain management planning.
concepts.definition: Supply chain management planning encompasses three primary levels -- strategic, tactical, and operational -- each addressing different time horizons and decision scopes to align supply with demand.
concepts.explanation: Strategic planning is a long-term approach that defines the overall supply chain direction, including supplier selection, warehouse placement, and market alignment, typically reviewed annually. Tactical planning is a mid-range approach that sets production, inventory, and distribution priorities over a medium-term horizon such as a quarter or seasonal cycle, often referred to as master planning. Operational planning focuses on day-to-day execution including inventory management, logistics coordination, and performance monitoring. Together, these three levels ensure that supply chain activities are coordinated from high-level strategy down to daily operations.

sources.title: Mastering the Three Levels of Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Planning 101: From Basics to Best Practices
Supply Chain Management Concepts

relationships.description: Static and dynamic planning are approaches applied within these planning levels.
The master production schedule is a key output of tactical planning.

concepts.definition: Static planning uses a fixed plan generated at a point in time that remains unchanged until the next planning cycle, while dynamic planning continuously updates the plan as master data changes such as new orders or demand shifts.
concepts.explanation: A static plan is generated through a master planning calculation using current data and remains stable until the next scheduled regeneration. It serves as an operating plan that personnel such as purchasers and production planners use for daily decision-making. A dynamic plan starts with the same baseline but is updated every time master data changes, such as when a new sales order is created, allowing the company to monitor the changing order network and item availability in real time. The dynamic approach uses net change calculations for faster processing, while the static plan provides a consistent reference point for operational activities.

sources.title: Set up master plans for the deprecated planning engine
Static vs. Dynamic Supply Chains: The Path to Resilience
Static Route vs Dynamic Route Planning: A Practical Guide

relationships.description: Static and dynamic are planning approaches used within the broader types of SCM planning.
The master production schedule can be managed using either static or dynamic planning methods.

concepts.topic: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.
concepts.definition: A master production schedule (MPS) is a plan that specifies what products will be manufactured, in what quantities, and when, serving as the bridge between demand forecasts and detailed production planning.
concepts.explanation: The MPS is determined by taking into account demand forecasts, the sales and operations plan, current customer orders (backlog), available inventory, material availability, production capacity constraints, and management policies. It translates aggregate planning into a specific build schedule for individual end items expressed in configurations, quantities, and dates. The MPS drives material requirements planning (MRP) by defining what finished goods need to be produced, which in turn determines the raw materials and components required. Time fences are established to control changes to the schedule, ensuring stability in near-term production while allowing flexibility for longer-term adjustments. The planning horizon must be at least as long as the longest cumulative lead time for any item in the plan.

sources.title: Master production schedule
Master Production Schedule (MPS) Guide
Master Production Schedule (MPS) define in APICS Dictionary 11 Edition

relationships.description: The MPS is a key output of tactical-level supply chain planning.
The MPS uses the bill of materials to determine component and material requirements.
The MPS drives raw material procurement through material requirements planning.

concepts.definition: A bill of materials (BOM) is a comprehensive, structured list of all raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and the quantities of each needed to manufacture a finished product.
concepts.explanation: The BOM serves as the foundation for production planning systems, providing essential data for manufacturing resource planning, product costing, and material provisioning. A single-level BOM lists only the immediate components of a product, while a multi-level (indented) BOM shows the complete hierarchy including sub-assemblies and their constituent parts. BOMs are critical for material requirements planning (MRP), where the master production schedule is exploded through the BOM to determine what materials need to be purchased and when. They also support inventory planning, scheduling, and cost estimation across the supply chain.

sources.title: Bills of Material (BOM) In the Supply Chain
What Is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? The Complete Guide to BOMs
Bill of Materials (BOM) in Supply Chain Management: Impacts and Examples

relationships.description: The BOM is used to explode the MPS into detailed material requirements.
The BOM specifies the raw materials needed for production.
Sub-assemblies listed in a BOM become work-in-process during manufacturing.

concepts.topic: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.
concepts.definition: Raw materials are the unprocessed or minimally processed inputs -- such as steel, wood, plastics, and agricultural products -- that are used in the production of goods and represent the first link in the supply chain.
concepts.explanation: Raw materials procurement is the starting point of the production chain and influences every subsequent stage from manufacturing to distribution. They are classified as either direct materials, which become part of the finished product (such as steel in an automobile), or indirect materials, which support the production process (such as lubricants and cleaning agents). Effective raw materials inventory management involves ordering, storing, monitoring, and inspecting resources throughout the production cycle to prevent shortages that cause production delays while avoiding excess stock that ties up capital. Accurate tracking of raw materials is essential for smooth production flow, timely customer order fulfillment, and cost optimization across the supply chain.

sources.title: What Are Raw Materials?
Raw Materials Inventory: Explanation, Benefits, and Tips
Raw Material - Definition, Importance, Types & Example

relationships.description: The BOM specifies which raw materials are required and in what quantities.
Raw materials become work-in-process once they enter the production process.
Raw materials are transformed through production into finished goods.

concepts.topic: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.
concepts.definition: Work-in-process (WIP) refers to partially completed goods that are in various stages of the manufacturing process, having moved beyond raw materials but not yet completed as finished goods.
concepts.explanation: WIP inventory includes all materials, labor, and manufacturing overhead that have been invested in products that are still being assembled or processed on the production floor. It is classified as a current asset on the balance sheet under the inventory category, distinct from raw materials and finished goods. Managing WIP efficiently is critical because high WIP levels indicate bottlenecks, longer lead times, and increased carrying costs, while low WIP levels suggest a lean and responsive production process. WIP is calculated as beginning WIP inventory plus production costs minus the cost of finished goods produced. Effective WIP management requires coordination between production, procurement, and logistics functions to maintain smooth material flow through the manufacturing process.

sources.title: Work in process
The Monthly Metric: Work-in-Process Inventory
What is Work in Process?

relationships.description: Raw materials become work-in-process once they enter manufacturing.
Work-in-process becomes finished goods upon completion of manufacturing.
The master production schedule drives the flow of WIP through the production process.

concepts.topic: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.
concepts.definition: Finished goods are products that have completed the entire manufacturing process, passed quality inspection, and are ready for sale and delivery to customers.
concepts.explanation: Finished goods inventory represents the final stage of the production pipeline, where items are fully assembled, inspected, and stored in warehouses awaiting distribution. The primary goal is to move finished goods to customers as quickly and efficiently as possible, minimizing holding costs while meeting customer demand and service level targets. Finished goods inventory acts as a buffer to absorb demand fluctuations, preventing stockouts during unexpected surges while avoiding excess inventory that ties up working capital and storage space. Effective finished goods management balances production output with distribution logistics to optimize order fulfillment, reduce lead times, and maintain customer satisfaction throughout the supply chain.

sources.title: Finished Goods Inventory: What It Is, Formula, and Examples

relationships.description: Work-in-process becomes finished goods upon completion of manufacturing.
Finished goods are the end result of transforming raw materials through production.
The master production schedule determines what finished goods will be produced and when.
